metadata.summary: The PM as integrator ensures that decisions in scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, resources, communications, and procurement are consistent and mutually reinforcing.

content.title: Project Integration Management
content.summary: The PM as integrator ensures that decisions in scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, resources, communications, and procurement are consistent and mutually reinforcing. All component plans live as typed YAML/JSON artifacts in git; status RAG is computed from measurable variance thresholds, never from opinion; and every baseline change goes through a documented change-control PR. Agents propose; sponsors approve baseline changes.

why.p: Silo management — optimising each knowledge area independently — routinely produces locally good and globally bad outcomes: a scope addition that is "free" for engineering adds cost and schedule risk that the PM discovers two weeks later. Integration is the discipline that catches cross-area effects before they become surprises. Without an integration view, status reports reflect the best news from each silo rather than the worst constraint across them.

list.item: Multi-team / multi-vendor programs where decisions in one area routinely affect another
Regulated programs where the Project Charter is a contractual artifact requiring version control
Hybrid agile+waterfall environments with component plans in different tools needing a single source of truth
Portfolio PMO reporting where dozens of projects feed the same status rollup
Integrated Change Control when a change request affects more than one baseline
Solo or duo teams — overhead exceeds value; a one-page README and a kanban board cover integration
Pure agile single-team with one product backlog — Scrum already integrates work; bolting on PMBoK integration creates conflict
Pre-charter exploratory spikes — formalising integration too early kills learning
Portfolios where project owners refuse to share artifacts in a common format — fix governance first

section.name: project-charter-elements
section.p: Rules for authoring a project charter that is legally valid as a contractual artifact, version-controlled, and re-baselined only through approved change requests.

rule.statement: A project charter must include: project purpose, SMART objectives, high-level scope (in/out), named success criteria with baseline and target, key stakeholders with roles, PM authority level, and high-level budget and timeline. Missing any element makes the charter unenforceable.
rule.rationale: Charters without measurable success criteria degenerate into "on time, on budget, on scope" — meaningless without baselines. Every element must be present before formal authorization.

rule.statement: Success criteria must be SMART: specific metric, current baseline, target value, and by-date. Reject "improve customer satisfaction" — require "NPS from 32 to 45 by Q3 2026".
rule.rationale: Vague success criteria enable scope creep and make project closure decisions impossible. Every criterion must answer: how will we know we succeeded?

rule.statement: Store the charter in git as charter.yaml, signed (Signed-off-by or digital signature reference), and tagged baseline-v1 at authorization. Re-baseline only via approved change requests with sponsor sign-off recorded in git history.
rule.rationale: Charters that drift from reality without version history cannot be audited. A PDF filed and never reconciled is not a controlled document.

rule.statement: Re-baseline triggers: scope change greater than 10%, schedule slip greater than 15%, cost overrun greater than 10%, or sponsor change. Anything below these thresholds is managed via the change log without a full re-baseline.
rule.rationale: Frequent re-baselines reset performance measurement and hide variance history. Reserve re-baselining for genuine scope shifts that make the original baseline meaningless.

section.name: rag-computation-rules
section.p: Deterministic RAG computation rules from EVM metrics, change control workflow as git PRs, and integration antipatterns.

rule.statement: Compute RAG from measurable thresholds, never from opinion. Schedule: GREEN if SPI >= 0.95, YELLOW 0.85-0.95, RED below 0.85. Cost: GREEN if CPI >= 0.95, YELLOW 0.85-0.95, RED below 0.85. Overall: worst single dimension.
rule.rationale: Opinion-based status defaults to GREEN because pessimistic framing is rarer in training data and in human psychology. Threshold-based computation removes the bias.

rule.statement: Missing data is YELLOW with reason, not GREEN. If a required metric is null, surface it as a gap rather than assuming everything is on track.
rule.rationale: LLMs return GREEN when data is absent; the correct response is to flag the missing input as a monitoring gap requiring investigation.

section.reference: Deterministic RAG from EVM + risk YAML baselines

rule.statement: Every change request must traverse all 8 component plans (scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, resources, comms, procurement) and emit an impact matrix before approval. Standalone scope edits without schedule and cost re-estimates are rejected.
rule.rationale: Silo approval of scope changes is the primary mechanism by which integration fails. A scope addition that "won't affect timeline" always affects timeline — the PM just hasn't computed it yet.

rule.statement: Run change control as a PR: CR description, cross-area impact matrix, baseline patch proposal, sponsor approval, merge tagged baseline-vN+1. Every baseline change must have a unique CR ID and immutable diff in git history.
rule.rationale: Audit trails are mandatory in regulated programs. A git-based change control system provides immutable history with timestamps and approvers.

rule.statement: Agents have zero authority to approve baseline changes. The agent proposes baseline patches; the sponsor or change board approves via their recorded sign-off before merge.
rule.rationale: Autonomous baseline changes by agents constitute an audit risk and a governance failure. Human approval is non-negotiable for any change touching cost or schedule baselines.

antipattern.description: Agent drafts charter with fabricated stakeholder names, dollar figures, and milestone dates.
antipattern.reason: All numeric and named values must come from explicit input fields; never let the agent infer or fill in charter data from context. Charter hallucinations become contractual commitments.

antipattern.description: Agent edits a charter mid-flight without a change request reference.
antipattern.reason: Charter freeze is a governance discipline. Enforce with branch protection and CODEOWNERS; any edit without an approved CR ID in the commit message is rejected.

antipattern.description: Component plans in heterogeneous tools (Jira for tasks, Smartsheet for schedule, Excel for budget) without a normalised export layer.
antipattern.reason: Without a common format, the integration agent cannot compute cross-area impact. Require each plan owner to export to a canonical YAML schema at each status cycle.

antipattern.description: Status report generation that leaks vendor pricing, customer names, or employee performance into a shared report.
antipattern.reason: Component plans often contain commercially sensitive and personally identifiable information. Run a scrubber on the integration report before publishing to broad channels.

antipattern.description: Charters drift from reality if not version-controlled; many orgs sign a PDF, file it, and never reconcile against the live plan.
antipattern.reason: Version control with git tags enables audits and baseline resets. A PDF filed once and never updated is a liability in regulated programs.

antipattern.description: Integrated change control becomes a bottleneck when CAB / CCB meets infrequently — agile tempo collides with waterfall governance.
antipattern.reason: Async PR-based change control with async approval (e.g., 24-hour approval SLA) scales better than synchronous CAB meetings.

antipattern.description: LLMs producing status reports tend toward "GREEN washing" unless forced to compute RAG from quantitative inputs (variance thresholds).
antipattern.reason: Threshold-based RAG computation is the only reliable antidote to LLM optimism bias in status reporting.

antipattern.description: The PM-as-integrator model assumes the PM has access; in matrix orgs functional managers gate visibility and the integration view is incomplete.
antipattern.reason: Integration requires transparency across all 8 areas. Matrix orgs need a central repository (git, Confluence, SharePoint) where all plans are visible to the PM role, not just to function leads.

antipattern.description: Charter "success criteria" often degenerate into "on time, on budget, on scope" — meaningless without baselines and measurable outcomes.
antipattern.reason: Success criteria must be SMART and tied to business value, not just schedule/cost/scope conformance. "NPS 45 by Q3" is measurable; "increase revenue" is not.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Treat each component plan as a typed YAML/JSON artifact in git: charter.yaml, scope-baseline.yaml, schedule-baseline.yaml, cost-baseline.yaml, quality-plan.yaml, resource-plan.yaml, risk-register.yaml, comms-plan.yaml, procurement-plan.yaml. An integration subagent reads all baselines, computes RAG status from variance thresholds, and emits the weekly Project Status Report deterministically. A change-control subagent intercepts every change request, runs cross-area impact analysis, and proposes baseline patches that humans approve. The agent never auto-approves changes touching cost or schedule baselines — those require sponsor sign-off recorded in git.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent — drives integration as SDD tasks (TASK_charter, TASK_baseline_lock, TASK_status_report, TASK_change_request_NN). Custom charter-author-agent (opus) — drafts charter from intake form + business case; flags missing SMART objectives and acceptance criteria. Custom status-aggregator-agent (sonnet) — reads all baseline files, EVM metrics, risk register; emits Status Report with computed RAG (no opinion-based GREEN). Custom change-impact-agent (opus) — for each change request, walks scope/schedule/cost/quality/risk/resources and emits an impact matrix plus baseline patch proposal. Custom plan-coherence-agent (sonnet) — runs after every plan update; catches misalignments (schedule task not in WBS, cost line without WBS code, risk without owner). password-scrubber-agent — runs over charter / status reports before commit; charters often contain sponsor names, vendor pricing, customer NDAs.

list.item: Charter is law — version it in git, sign it (digital sign or Signed-off-by:), and re-baseline only via approved change requests.
One source of truth per artifact: schedule lives in one tool, exported to YAML for integration; banning duplicate plans prevents drift.
Status RAG must be computed, never opinion-based. Define variance thresholds in .integration.yaml and have the agent enforce them.
Integrated Change Control runs through PRs (or equivalent) — CR description, impact matrix, baseline patch, sponsor approval, merge tagged baseline-vN+1.
Status reports go out on a calendar cadence; missed reports trigger automatic escalation, not silence.
Component plan owners are named in .CODEOWNERS so PRs route correctly; PM owns integration, not every plan.
Tie integration to lessons-learned — every approved CR generates a candidate lesson if it crossed >2 baselines.
Charter success criteria must be SMART and measurable; reject "improve customer satisfaction" without metric, baseline, target, and date.
Always pair scope changes with schedule and cost re-estimates in the same PR; refuse standalone scope edits.
Re-baseline triggers: scope change > 10%, schedule slip > 15%, cost overrun > 10%, or sponsor change.
LLMs default to "GREEN" status when data is missing — force null on missing inputs and compute RAG only from non-null values; missing data → YELLOW with reason.
Agents drafting charters fabricate stakeholders, dollar figures, and dates; require all numbers to come from explicit input fields, no inference.
Integrated change control without an audit trail breaks regulator audits; every CR must have a unique ID, immutable diff, and approver signature in git history.
Cross-baseline impact analysis hallucinates dependencies — feed the agent the actual dependency graph (WBS + schedule predecessors), do not let it infer.
Status report generation can leak confidential info (vendor pricing, customer names, employee performance) when sourced from raw plans; run scrubber before publishing.
Agents conflate "Project Manager authority" with "agent autonomy"; the agent has zero authority to approve baseline changes — it proposes, sponsor approves.
Reading multiple component plan files into one prompt explodes context; use a summary index and pull individual files on demand.
Charter freeze is a discipline gap: agents will happily edit a charter mid-flight; enforce with branch protection and CODEOWNERS.
Human-in-the-loop checkpoints (mandatory): charter approval, baseline lock, baseline change, scope/cost/schedule deltas, change-request approval, project closure.

content.reference: Deterministic RAG from EVM + risk YAML baselines

template.purpose: Deterministic RAG computation from EVM and risk YAML baselines

see-also.link: PMI PMBOK 7e — Project Integration Management; Performance Domain integration.
ISO 21500 / 21502 — Project management — Guidance on project, programme and portfolio management.
